g.data-narration: Welcome to System Calls! Today we'll explore how applications communicate securely with the operating system.
Operating systems use Dual-Mode: User Mode for apps (restricted) and Kernel Mode for OS (full hardware access).
A system call is the fundamental interface between user apps and the OS kernel - the ONLY entry point for privileged operations.
g.text: A programmed request to the OS kernel to perform a privileged task
Implemented as software interrupts (int 0x80) or syscall instruction

g.data-narration: Let's trace the journey of a system call: from API call through library, trigger trap, mode switch, kernel execution, and return.
Steps 1-3: Application calls library function (like read()), library prepares arguments, then executes special instruction triggering a trap.
g.text: CPU saves state and switches from User Mode → Kernel Mode

g.data-narration: Steps 4-6: CPU jumps to trap handler, kernel executes the system call (like sys_read), then returns result to user process.
g.text: CPU returns to User Mode - process resumes after trap instruction

g.data-narration: Process Control calls manage process lifecycle: fork() creates new process, exec() runs programs, exit() terminates, wait() pauses for children.
File Management calls handle files: open(), read(), write(), close(), and lseek() to position the file pointer for I/O operations.
g.text: Create, delete, read, write, and manipulate files

g.data-narration: Device Management calls interact with hardware: ioctl() controls devices, while read()/write() work on device files for I/O operations.
g.text: Request and release devices, read/write to them

g.data-narration: Information Maintenance calls get/set system data: getpid() returns process ID, gettimeofday() gets time, sysinfo() provides system info.
g.text: Get/set system data like time and process attributes

g.data-narration: Communication calls enable Inter-Process Communication: pipe() for pipes, shmget() for shared memory, msgget() for message queues.
Protection calls enforce security: chmod() changes permissions, chown() changes owner, setuid() sets user ID for access control.
API defines function interfaces (what to request), while System Call is the actual mechanism crossing user-kernel boundary (how to request).
g.text: API vs System Call: What's the Difference?
Example: printf() API wraps write() system call | sin() API uses NO system calls

g.data-narration: Here's how system calls appear in code: C's read() triggers sys_read, Python's os.getpid() calls getpid system call under the hood.
System calls provide security, hardware abstraction, and portability - the foundation of reliable and portable software.
Here's your quick reference: Six categories of system calls with their purposes and examples for exam preparation.
g.text: Summary: All 6 Categories at a Glance

g.data-narration: Remember: API is interface definition (what), system call is implementation (how). They're related but distinct concepts for your exam.
g.text: ✓ System Call = ONLY entry to kernel

g.data-narration: Great job! You've mastered system calls - from dual-mode operation to the six categories. You're ready for your OS exam!
